10.1016/j.jmp.2025.102937
No data was used for the research described in the article.

A typical redundant signals experiment assesses response time (RT) performance when either of two (or more) presented signals is sufficient to make a correct response against response time to respond to either of the signals alone. In some studies perception of both signals takes place faster than either alone and even faster than what a parallel system with independent, unlimited capacity channels can predict. This behavior is referred to as super capacity ( Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ). In fact, J. Miller’s earlier  1982  data was interpreted as exhibiting performance that was so super capacity that it violated an upper bound on performance through a statistic known in mathematics as the Poisson Inequality, and now referred to in the literature as the race model inequality (RMI). The most popular type of explanatory model for these patterns assumes that information (treated as an activation random variable in each channel) from two parallel channels is summed into a subsequent single channel where the sum is compared with a criterion activation. Such a process is dubbed coactivation and several specific such models were shown to be able to account for RMI violations.  Townsend and Nozawa (1995)  later demonstrated that  any  arbitrary counter model (i.e., with arbitrary stochastic processes for the concurrent counters) perforce predicted violation of the RMI. Further, using the systems factorial technology statistical function named the survivor interaction contrast (SIC), which will be described below, they proved that standard Poisson counter models predict a specific distinctive function for coactive processing. However, they were able to derive the SIC signatures for other architectures (e.g., parallel and serial models) which were entirely general rather than being confined to Poisson counters. Subsequently,  Houpt and Townsend (2011)  generalized the SIC prediction for coactive models to the popular Ratcliff–Wiener drift–diffusion process. Our satisfied goal in the current work was to further extend the classes of coactive systems associated with this same SIC shape to the popular Linear Ballistic Accumulator (e.g.,  Brown & Heathcote, 2008 ), the entire class of Inhomogeneous Poisson Processes, and a novel system we call the bare bones stochastic process. In addition, we adduce a set of sufficient conditions that any monotone distribution should meet to predict for their convolution to obey the canonical SIC form.

The SIC functions on response times form a critical role in the experimental identification of mental (cognitive, perceptual, action, etc.) architectures combined with the pertinent decisional stopping rule as well as workload capacity ( Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ). 
                         1 
                      
                      
                         1 
                         Another axiom, not always assumed by all investigators, assumes that, by definition, the (probability distributions of) channels of a coactive system are invariant as to whether or not other channels are in operation (and therefore feeding into the common outlet). This provision is called “context invariance” and has strong implications for speed of processing as measure by the capacity coefficient ( Colonius, 1990; Townsend & Eidels, 2011; Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ). However, in this study, we always assume that both channels in the coactive system are present and therefore do not require this axiom.

Recall that the survivor function of a response time is the complement of the cumulative distribution function (i.e.,

For the classic results on identifying mental architectures to hold, selective influence of the factors on their respective subsystems must operate at least at the echelon of distribution ordering ( Townsend, 1990a; Townsend & Liu, 2022 ). When selective influence holds, these continuous functions of times represent highly distinctive signatures of the various architectures plus stopping rule. The original predictions for the classical serial and parallel systems, along with the most popular stopping rules, are entirely distribution free. That is, the predictions are true for any stochastic processes obeying the definition of these architectures plus stopping rule. This paper will not go into detail regarding serial and parallel systems. The reader interested in more detail on those is referred to tutorials such as  Algom et al. (2015) ,  Townsend et al. (2018) ,  Schweickert et al. (2012)  or compendia containing theory and applications such as  Little et al. (2017) .

The case for coactive systems is more multifaceted and even thorny. To begin with, parallel and serial processing can be defined in a descriptive fashion, placing definitions on joint distributions of processing times. In contrast, coactive models are characterized through distributions on state spaces from the ground up. We here focus on investigating broader classes of coactive systems than have been covered heretofore.

We begin with a definition stated colloquially but rigorously nonetheless.

Definition 1 
                         A coactive system is one in which two or more probabilistically independent parallel stochastic processes feed their activation into a summation channel. The activation of this combined channel is then instantaneously compared with a decision bound. At the first time this bound is equaled or exceeded, the decision is made followed by the required response.

A coactive system is one in which two or more probabilistically independent parallel stochastic processes feed their activation into a summation channel. The activation of this combined channel is then instantaneously compared with a decision bound. At the first time this bound is equaled or exceeded, the decision is made followed by the required response.

Fig. 1  exhibits the architecture of a typical coactive system. The reader will observe that this definition, like those presented elsewhere for parallel and serial systems, is completely general with regard to distribution. However, as just observed, the definition contains a statement about the state space. The relevant theorem for coactive systems in  Townsend and Nozawa (1995)  was confined to Poisson counting distributions with their twin gamma distributions on waiting (i.e., finishing) times. Somewhat later,  Houpt and Townsend (2011)  generalized the SIC prediction to superposition of Wiener–Levy processes with drift. These results begin to suggest that the original signature discovered by  Townsend and Nozawa (1995)  might be reasonably pervasive. We here provide further extension of these theoretical findings to yet other distributions outside those studied so far.

The first model we consider is the popular LBA, linear ballistic accumulator ( Brown & Heathcote, 2008 ), demonstrating through  Theorem 2.1  that it follows the canonical form of coactive SIC curves. Subsequently, we take up the class known as inhomogeneous Poisson processes (for the state, or equivalently inhomogeneous gamma processes referring to the time); hereafter referred to as IPPs. In the succeeding section, we delve deeply into quite important characteristics of general coactive systems founded on monotone increasing activation (state space) systems. Finally, we propose a new stochastic process which we dub the “bare bones accumulator model”. This model is introduced in a descriptive fashion meaning its probability law can be stated without committing to a particular type of (activation) state space. However, we show that it can be endowed with more than one underlying state space.

At its core, the LBA is a race model based on independent accumulators that have constant rates of accumulation which vary randomly from trial to trial.  Fig. 2(a)  captures the basic aspects of such models in primal form and  Fig. 2(b)  shows how the model can expand to include coactivation.

Additional variability across trials is included in the model in the form of variation in the amount of accumulation needed to elicit a response. Usually, this latter form of variability is assumed to be due to variation in the starting level of the accumulator while the threshold,  
                            b 
                         , is assumed to be constant across trials. The standard formulation assumes that the start-point is uniformly distributed, with a range between  
                            0 
                          and  
                            A 
                          and that the variation in accumulation rates is normally distributed with mean  
                            ν 
                          and standard deviation  
                            s 
                         .

Fig. 3  gives an exemplary coactive SIC function of the LBA type.

Next, we turn our attention to the important class of Inhomogeneous Poisson Processes, which are less commonly used explicitly in psychological science, but are rather general, hence serve our purpose of expanding the generality of the coactive SIC shape.

Inhomogeneous Poisson processes (again, IPPs), due to historical developments, have not been employed in mathematical psychology to the extent of Wiener processes with drift (initiated and researched for decades by  Ratcliff (1978) , and colleagues which, as mentioned, have been shown to possess canonical SIC functions in  Houpt and Townsend (2011)  or somewhat less by discrete-time, continuous-state random walks ( Link, 1982; Link & Heath, 1975; Smith & Vickers, 1988; Vickers, 1978 ) or even standard Poisson counters (e.g.,  Townsend and Ashby 1983 ; with regard to state-space but not time, see  Pike 1971, 1973 ). However, they have received some modest degree of serious attention in psychological science (see, e.g.,  Smith & Van Zandt, 2000; Townsend & Liu, 2022 ).

There are several possible starting points that characterize IPPs. And, even this rather global set of processes can be extended to an even broader scope such as general point processes (see, e.g.,  Chiu et al., 2013 ).

For our limited purposes, we can rely on a very nice property indeed of this class: The formulas for both the probability mass on number of events (say random variable  
                            K 
                         ) in an interval of time and the probability density of the waiting time for those  
                            K 
                          events to happen, are both identical in form to those in the case of homogeneous Poisson (or gamma) processes.

To remind the reader, for homogeneous Poisson processes, the vital formula for the probability of  
                            
                               K 
                               = 
                               k

Before the next step where we take into account a coactive mechanism, we pause to take notice of the generality of IPPs.

Another felicitous aspect of IPPs, is that when we superpose two or more such processes, as when parallel channels feed their counts into a final conduit, the resulting stochastic process can be expressed as a single IPP with a ‘rate’ that is simply the sum of the rates from the contributing channels!

                            A

                            B

We are now in a position to generalize the  Townsend and Nozawa (1995)  principle to this much more general model.

Theorem 2.2 
                            
                               All IPPs obey the canonical SIC signature: Their SIC signature starts negative and then goes positive, crossing the 0-line but once.

All IPPs obey the canonical SIC signature: Their SIC signature starts negative and then goes positive, crossing the 0-line but once.

The domain of  
                                  t 
                               , where this function is negative or positive determines the form of the mixed second difference of the survivor function, that is the SIC function signature.

Thus, to satisfy the canonical SIC form, this expression should be negative for  
                                  t 
                                close to 0 and become positive and stay there as  
                                  t 
                                grows larger. By assumption, the partial derivatives of

                                           t 
                                           , 
                                           b 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                are both positive for all  
                                  t 
                               . Further, the next two terms in the product are also positive for all  
                                  t 
                                (the exponential function is positive valued and each of the terms in the next ratio are positive). Hence, as complex as this function at first appears, its sign is totally governed by,

Theorem 2.2  establishes that the IPP model exhibits the canonical shape of the SIC function. However, another valuable attribute of the coactive signature, one which differentiates it from that for exhaustive serial processing, is that the early negative part of the SIC is smaller in area than the subsequent negative portion. We now confirm that this holds for the special case ( Example 2.2 ) of the IPP class where the intensity function is a power function of t.

Lemma 2.2 
                            
                               The early negative part of the SIC function for IPP based on power functions, is outweighed by the subsequent positive part.

The early negative part of the SIC function for IPP based on power functions, is outweighed by the subsequent positive part.

Next, as observed before we really got underway, the convolutions within the present coactive models are considerably more subtle than is the case when we are adding time (as in exhaustive serial processing). So how do we obtain the result that the early double difference is negative (or the continuous analogue that the second mixed partial derivative is negative)? The key is found in the fact that for very small  
                            t 
                         , the difference in mass functions must cross as  
                            j 
                          goes from 0 to 1 to 2 and so on. That early negative difference is weighted by the survivor function (equivalently the cumulative distribution function on the number of counts) difference in the B channel in such a way as to out-weigh the earliest positive difference (e.g.,

Then a slightly different representation of the convolution takes into account all individual counts from A and B that sum to less than the criterion.                                = 
                               2 
                            
                          so the survivor function must consider all sums less than or equal to one.

                          and it turns out that this sum is negative and bigger in absolute value than the positive one just above, thereby again delivering the negative SIC result!

We now press forward towards a greater scope of processes underpinning a coactive type system. Naturally, we always retain the axiom-conditions confining us to stochastically independent sources summing their activations (states) into a common pool which perforce generates a first passage time or in psychological terms the first time of surmounting a decisional criterion or in many examples, completion time or stopping time.

Now, the set of all stochastic processes defined and worked on by stochastic theorists poses a significant challenge to our goals. Many of these, including most studied stochastic differential equations such as the set of diffusions, possess the property of both up and down fluctuations. This elemental detail straightaway exponentiates the challenges, at least in the great preponderance of instances, in finding distributions on the stopping time. Except in a few very special examples, the gravest difficulties appear when the trajectories (stochastic paths, etc.) possess some degree of continuity ( Smith & Van Zandt, 2000 ). But this obstacle implies that we cannot enlist the notion of a convolution of distributions placed into a functional equation to explore the form of our SIC functions.

The scenario brightens when we ask about processes which only increase. Now, naturally those are usually unreasonable when two decisions are mandatory, but just as always increasing discrete processes (any counting models or related systems with a counting underpinning such as compound Poisson processes with only positive jumps) can have much utility in our modeling science, so too might continuous-time, continuous-state (hereafter, doubly-continuous) processes that are monotone increasing or even processes that are partially continuous but also include points which are not differentiable or even include finite increasing jumps.

Interestingly, the famous Grice model ( Grice, 1968, 1972 ) as well as its offshoots such as the Linear Ballistic Accumulator ( Brown & Heathcote, 2008 ) and the Cascade Model ( Ashby, 1982; McClelland, 1979; Townsend & Wenger, 2021 ) are prime examples of the monotone variety and may well be the simplest non-trivial exemplars of this class. Other than those, we are unaware of other continuous state monotone models of information processing in psychology or cognitive science.

In the following, we employ Lebesgue integration and follow the common assumption that, when the processes include intervals of continuity interspersed with jumps, the processes are continuous from the right and possess limits from the left (the so called cadlag class). Although our current focus is on doubly-continuous (or at least piecewise continuous) processes, this allows our developments to apply to discrete state space models (e.g. counting processes) via the robustness of Lebesgue integration.

The foremost motivation for focusing on never-decreasing processes is: Because of the monotone property, we can comfortably obviate the usual technical impediment not-so-kindly tendered to first passage times (here, passage time equals stopping time and is the time up to the criterial state) by up-and-down diffusion processes. This means that we can ponder over a functional equation that is based on a convolution, for arbitrary  
                            t 
                         , of the probability of the sum of the accrued states from the two channels. The duality enjoyed by the Poisson processes, namely that the survivor function that

                               t

                               c

                            t 
                          can also be engaged with more general monotone processes. We take as our state space

Since our main target will be either mixtures of continuous intervals with jumps or almost surely continuous states, we will refer to our likelihood entities as “probability functions” or use similar general terms since a particular probability law may or may not admit densities.

Now, just as in continuous time, discrete state models, we know that for any fixed time  
                            t 
                         , the likelihood on  
                            x

                            x

                            t 
                          to evince the traditional negative blip in the SIC function. This type of structure permitted proofs of SIC theorems for the parallel vs. serial architecture in earlier work (e.g.,  Townsend & Nozawa, 1995; Townsend & Schweickert, 1985 ). We can only do one thing at a time but the reader will find that the succeeding section on the bare bones accumulator model will help exemplify the following reasoning.

First, we set down the foregoing assumptions in axiomatic fashion:

Axiom 1  is just a sufficient condition to order the A and B cumulative distributions ( Townsend, 1990b ) but this fact also aids in our further developments. Note that it does preclude some cases of multimodal distributions because multimodality can lead to multiple density crossings.  Axiom 2  is also relatively harmless: Most probability density (mass or simply measure) functions on  
                            x 
                          will move to the right for larger  
                            t 
                          and so will their cross-over point  
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                          and the same goes for the B distribution.  Axiom 3  is perhaps a bit more constraining, but nevertheless includes most accumulator models as this increase over time is a way to reflect the assumption that discriminability between choices increases with deliberation. We will return to the potential consequences of violating this assumption in the discussion.

Theorem 2.3 
                            
                               Together,  
                               Axioms 1 
                               ,  
                               2 
                               , and  
                               3  
                               suffice to produce a canonical SIC for coactive models: beginning with a negative notch for small  
                               
                                  t 
                                
                               and transitioning, for larger  
                               
                                  t 
                               
                               , to a positive section of the SIC which will be greater than the area of the earlier negative region.

In order to see how the above characteristics also emerge in the case of discrete state spaces, we work out a specific instance in the set of ordinary Poisson counters in  Appendix B . We next instantiate the above tenets and properties in a novel quite general stochastic process.

The new model is represented by a continuous or discrete state (but we concentrate on the continuous state version), continuous time and monotone stochastic process that possesses the above characteristics. We call it the “bare bones accrual model”.

Our BBAM model was purposely invented as a descriptive vehicle analogous to descriptive models for recognition and confusion. For example, Luce’s choice model and Townsend’s all-or-none model are termed “descriptive” because they lack an underlying stochastic (in time) process ( Luce, 1963; Townsend, 1971; Townsend & Landon, 1983 ). In the present case, the BB model can be characterized as descriptive due to the absence of its sample paths.

While the form of the BBAM was chosen based more on a balance between descriptive range and mathematical tractability, it is intriguing perhaps to learn that the class of sample paths may contain more than one member. Even more fascinating to us was the discovery that one such space belongs to the family popularly known as the previously discussed linear ballistic accumulator model (LBA), which we demonstrate after an exploration of the general BBAM.

                         x

                         x

                         t 
                      .

Our main goal is to demonstrate that the likelihood kernel (density in the presence of absolute continuity) of the convolution of two ‘channels’ following this type of distribution obeys the stricture in the corollary to Theorem III. We will thus begin to construct the various important facets of this family and then, when we arrive at the formula which pertains to this question, we will prove the condition indeed holds. Although the integrand is a fairly straightforward ratio of quadratic functions, its integral is starting to become more complicated hence, we have included it in  Appendix C  for the low–low condition rather than here in the main body. Nonetheless, it plots, as shown on the left of  Fig. 9 , as a fairly ordinary looking survivor function. An associated exemplary convolution density is shown on the right side.

Given this outline of some of the basic properties of the BBAM, we now turn to the question of what will the SIC prediction for this simple stochastic process be?

Theorem 3.1 
                         
                            The BBAM predicts canonical SIC curves.

The BBAM predicts canonical SIC curves.

                                     )

                               c

                             Recall that the crossing point of probability functions on the A channel is  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     t 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  =

                                           ) 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                           
                                              4 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  t 
                               
                            , so that the difference of the probability functions is positive for  
                               
                                  x 
                                  < 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     t 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             and negative thereafter. For small values of  
                               
                                  t

                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     t 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             will be far below  
                               c 
                             and  
                               
                                  Δ 
                                  
                                     
                                        p

                                     x 
                                     , 
                                     T 
                                     = 
                                     t 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     ] 
                                  
                               
                             will cross to negative early on. However, as  
                               t 
                             grows larger so does  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     t 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             and the positive area under  
                               
                                  Δ 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             is stretched out (though of course, being a density and/or mass function, the total mass remains constant). For  
                               t 
                             even bigger,  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     t 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  > 
                                  c 
                               
                             and since  
                               
                                  d 
                                  x 
                               
                             only integrates up to  
                               c 
                            , from there on, there are only positive contributions to the convolution integral.

                             So, as  
                               t 
                             grows, not only do we approach the point where the A channel likelihood difference only yields positive quantities to the convolution integral but the B channel difference in probabilities is moving its mass from the right to the left magnifying more and more the positive part of  
                               
                                  Δ 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            . The outcome is the canonical SIC shape. □

Fig. 10  reveals the above statements in a intuitive way with  
                         
                            Δ 
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  a 
                               
                            
                         
                       in red, the B channel survivor difference in blue and the product (i.e., the integrand in the convolution) in black. The first plot shows for small  
                         t 
                       the early crossover of  
                         
                            Δ 
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  a 
                               
                            
                         
                       and how the channel B contribution is almost 0 up until  
                         
                            c 
                            − 
                            x 
                         
                       is very small ( 
                         x 
                      , large) and so the black part is virtually 0. The next plot shows, for moderate  
                         t 
                      , what happens when there are both positive and negative contributions from  
                         
                            Δ 
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  a 
                               
                            
                         
                       but the negative part is accentuated by the heavy weighting from  
                         
                            Δ 
                            
                               
                                  P 
                               
                               
                                  b 
                               
                            
                         
                       (the blue curve). Finally, the third plot exhibits a case where  
                         t 
                       puts the crossover point close to  
                         c 
                       and the entire integrand only produces positive values to the convolution.

What remains is to figure out sample functions for this stochastic process (without coactivation) and to actually exhibit a computed SIC function for this model. We elect to perform the latter task first. The survivor function for the low–low condition is shown in  C . The other three have the same form. It would be beyond most humans to perform such computations in their life time, as this and it is perhaps surprising that such complex convolution distributions could follow from such an apparently simple model.  Fig. 11  shows some examples of the individual survivor functions, revealing their adherence to the proper orderings.  Fig. 12  shows an example of the resultant canonical SIC function.

We next turn to the demonstration that the BBAM encompasses the LBA model. Let us start with a slightly more general class and then devolve to LBA. It may be recalled from our earlier development that the LBA models predict the canonical SIC form.

It has been kind of a theoretical fly-in-the-ointment that the SIC properties of coactivation models have failed to enjoy the kind of high generality as parallel and serial models. The present investigation pushes, we believe, the boundaries of the class of coactive models which bear canonical SIC profiles significantly outward.

We learned that the set of popular LBA (Linear Ballistic Accumulator) models and that of IPPs (Inhomogeneous Poisson Counters) and with several exemplary special cases scrutinized, exhibit the canonical SIC structure. Several models are also proven to evince greater positive than negative portions of their SIC curves which of course also establishes that the mean interaction contrast results display a positive interaction.

We then embarked on a more in-depth exploration of general coactive theory founded on increasing (with time) monotone state-activation functions. These developments produced a set of quite universal sufficient conditions for this class. Finally, a new rather simple stochastic process, embodied in the bare-bones accumulator model, was put forth and employed to instantiate and expose the principles from  Theorem 2.2 .

In sum we witness an expanding set of coactive models that, when their architecture is probed through systems factorial technology, reveal canonical SIC curves.

It is appropriate to conclude with a set of caveats:

First, as seen in the computational examples of the BBAM, under certain circumstances, the negative part of the coactive SIC curve might be difficult or impossible to espy. Second, as  Eidels et al. (2011)  demonstrate, if the critical principle of selective influence fails in a significant way, other architectures than coactive might produce the shape associated with coactivity. At the simplest level, interactive parallel models can sometimes make such predictions. And, of course, more complex networks might also eventuate mimicking of simpler systems (e.g.,  Schweickert et al., 2012 ). To be sure, as the  Eidels et al. (2011)  investigation reveals, bringing to bear both the SIC functions together with capacity analyses can very much aid systems identification.

To end, given the somewhat intricate nature of the inherent processes embedded in coactive models, we are not ready to claim, as we could in the case with ordinary serial and parallel processing, that all coactive models obey this dictum. It could turn out that vagaries such as negative skew, multi-modality, etc., might emerge as counter examples. However, it does increasingly appear that the classical feature of S-shaped with smaller-negative-than-positive-areas (in sequence) adheres to a rather extensive set of such models.

                                  ϕ 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              b 
                                              − 
                                              t 
                                              ν 
                                           
                                           
                                              t 
                                              s 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        t 
                                     
                                     
                                        s 
                                        A 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ϕ 
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    b 
                                                    − 
                                                    t 
                                                    ν 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    t 
                                                    s 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        − 
                                        ϕ 
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    b 
                                                    − 
                                                    A 
                                                    − 
                                                    t 
                                                    ν 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    t 
                                                    s 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        t 
                                     
                                     
                                        s 
                                        A 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ϕ 
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    b 
                                                    − 
                                                    t 
                                                    ν 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    t 
                                                    s 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        − 
                                        ϕ 
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    b 
                                                    − 
                                                    t 
                                                    ν 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    t 
                                                    s 
                                                 
                                              
                                              − 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    A 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    t 
                                                    s

Hence, the sign of the SIC is determined by the sign of the difference of a standard normal distribution evaluated at two points, with the first point always greater than the second (because  
                         
                            A 
                            / 
                            t 
                            s 
                         
                       is always positive). Thus, for early times, both points are positive, but the second point is less positive, meaning the value of the standard normal distribution is higher at the second point and the overall SIC is then negative. The SIC is zero when the two points are equidistant from 0. Because the distance between the two points is  
                         
                            A 
                            / 
                            t 
                            s 
                         
                      , this implies that each point is  
                         
                            A 
                            / 
                            2 
                            t 
                            s 
                         
                       from 0. In particular,  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        b 
                                        − 
                                        t 
                                        ν 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        s 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                        t 
                                        s 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⇒ 
                                  t 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        b 
                                        − 
                                        A 
                                        / 
                                        2 
                                     
                                     
                                        ν 
                                     
                                  
                                  . 
                               
                            
                         
                      For any  
                         t 
                       larger than that value, the SIC must then be positive.

In this section, we demonstrate  Theorem 2.3  applied to a coactive model with a Poisson distribution on the state space. We assume that the changes from  
                         
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               l 
                            
                         
                       to  
                         
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               h 
                            
                         
                       only affect the rate of the  
                         A 
                       distribution (and similarly for  
                         B 
                      , thus the mass functions on the state space are given by,  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                        
                                           A

                                           ; 
                                           t

                       is an increasing function of

                       ( Axiom 2 ).

Thus, because the conditions of  Theorem 2.3  hold, the Poisson coactive model will have the canonical SIC shape.
